The original purpose of separating the `QueueWriter` from the `QueueReader` was, surprise, to separate the responsibilities of reading and writing to the queue. The classes didn't talk directly to each other; rather, each had a reference to opposite ends of the queue.

It looks like you've decided to move the responsibility of writing to the queue to the `QueueReader` and create a direct reference between them. What was your motivation for doing that?

In any case, the `QueueWriter` doesn't do very much now, and `QueueReader` is now a rather misleading name. It might make sense to just bin the `QueueWriter` altogether, and rename `QueueReader` to something like `Inbox`.

When we ask filter if it has seen an element and filter answers yes, the yes answer may be false positive. This could be caused by the collision of the hash value although it happens very rarely in our case. So asserting `char in new_bloom` could possible be false positive too theoretically, and we do not know when the hash values get clashed, so using `in` keyword here causing the test to be nondeterministic. 

I am aware that some existing tests do the same thing, it is due to some historical reasons, I will fix them. 

`bloom_one` will get assigned with `['n', 'o', 'p', 'q', 'r', 's', 't', 'u', 'v', 'w', 'x', 'y', 'z']`, and `bloom_two` will get assigned with ` ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e', 'f', 'g', 'h', 'i', 'j', 'k', 'l', 'm']`. 2 filters here have no dupe elements in themselves, and elements in one filter are completely differ from elements in another. I wonder would it be better if we check `new_bloom.count` is the sum of the counts of `bloom_one` and `bloom_two`?

The reason I did this was that I needed a handler to the event emitter. By creating the class in the handler, I get a handle to the base resource class and if I subclass from that and call ``super()`` on its ``__init__`` I will get an event emitter.

Ideally, I would add the event emitter to the event, but unfortunately, I cannot because the event emitter at that event is for the factory so if I register it to that emitter the higher level abstraction will be applied to all future clients that are created even though it is only called when the dynamodb resource is created.

I could also take it out of the method and subclass it from ``ServiceResource``, but it may need to be updated in the future if we ever build more base abstractions on top of ``ServiceResource``. However for now, this is completely viable way of doing it.

Notes

I think we want to issue the warn and move on to the next line. We're doing line-based processing, so it's feasible that one-line could go bad without impacting the other lines (for whatever reason) - we should be robust to that. If we get a `ValueError` here, we have nothing to yield back to iterator - there are no values we can draw from the line. I don't think we need to differentiate here between the errors unless further experience suggests otherwise - bad JSON value or incomplete line, the line output should give us enough information. Im using "segment" to denote this kind of output (with 1-length segments): (0, 1), (1, 2), (2, 3), (3, 4), etc.

Then there is the fromBlock -> toBlock type ranges, which the equivalent of above would be: (0, 0), (1, 1), (2, 2), etc.

Because the fromBlock/toBlock type ranges seem a little less conventional, I decided to make the basic function output the more conventional "segment" ranges (where given a range (a, b): b - a = step), and making them conform to the fromBlock/toBlock expected values as a separate step when iterating over the segments.

Chunker is confusing, because it is the same concept as segmenting.  When Im dealing with the block type ranges, I'll call them, "block ranges".

At first I was going to suggest using [`os.fspath`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/os.html#os.fspath), but then I remembered that that's only added in 3.6. We could skip this check on 3.5, but... we probably want to support `trio.Path` objects even on 3.5. And then I looked again at trio's async file I/O code, and we actually have a second copy of this `os.fspath` emulation there.

Would you be up to refactoring this into a `trio._util.fspath` helper? Ideally [based on the sample code here](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0519/#os)? (It turns out that calling `address.__fspath__()` directly is subtly wrong, because python's magic method lookup is weird.)
